parts.prompt:
A student combusts a sample of ascorbic acid, x y x, to determine its chemical
CO $H_2O$
composition. The only products of the reaction are 0.2400 mol of 2 and 2.883 g of 2 .
$H_2O$
i. Calculate the number of moles of 2 produced.
ii. The mole ratio of carbon (C) to oxygen (O) is 1:1 in ascorbic acid. Based on this
information and your answer to part A (i), determine the empirical formula of ascorbic
acid.
parts.scoring_notes: Part (a)(i): moles of H₂O = 2.883 g ÷ 18.02 g/mol = 0.1600 mol. Part (a)(ii): empirical formula is C₃H₄O₃ (or accept CHO if not fully reduced — but C₃H₄O₃ is the correct empirical formula for ascorbic acid given C:O = 1:1 and 0.2400 mol C and 0.3200 mol H atoms).

scoring_points.description: For the correct empirical formula consistent with the combustion data: 1 point

required_elements: Moles of H₂O = $\frac{2.883\text{ g}}{18.02\text{ g/mol}} = 0.1600$ mol
Moles of H = $2 \times 0.1600 = 0.3200$ mol; moles of C = 0.2400 mol; moles of O = 0.2400 mol (given C:O = 1:1)
alternatives.correct_example:
$n(\text{H}_2\text{O}) = \frac{2.883\text{ g}}{18.02\text{ g/mol}} = 0.1600\text{ mol}$ 
$n(\text{H}) = 2 \times 0.1600 = 0.3200\text{ mol}$ 
$n(\text{C}) = 0.2400\text{ mol}$; since C:O = 1:1, $n(\text{O}) = 0.2400\text{ mol}$ 
Ratio: C : H : O = 0.2400 : 0.3200 : 0.2400 = 3 : 4 : 3 
Empirical formula: $\text{C}_3\text{H}_4\text{O}_3$

wrong_examples: Using moles of CO₂ directly as moles of H₂O.
Forgetting that each mole of H₂O contains 2 moles of H atoms.
common_traps: Not applying the C:O = 1:1 constraint to determine oxygen content separately.

parts.prompt:
Ascorbic acid,
HAsc(aq),
acts as a weak acid, as shown in the equation.
HAsc(aq)+ $H_2O$(l) E $H_2O$+(aq)+ Asc-(aq)
The following titration curve was produced when a 10.0 mL sample of
HAsc(aq)
was titrated
using
0.0550
M
NaOH(aq).
i. Calculate the molar concentration of the ascorbic acid solution.
ii. From the titration curve, determine the approximate $pK_a$ of ascorbic acid.
Asc-
iii. What is the value of the ratio 7 A when the pH of the solution is 4.7?
HAsc

DHAsc
parts.scoring_notes: Part (b)(i): equivalence point volume read from graph ≈ 8.0 mL; M(HAsc) = (0.0550)(0.008)/(0.010). Part (b)(ii): pKa ≈ pH at half-equivalence point (4 mL on graph) ≈ 4.7. Part (b)(iii): when pH = pKa, [Asc⁻]/[HAsc] = 1.

scoring_points.description: For correct answers to (b)(i), (b)(ii), and (b)(iii) consistent with the graph: 1 point

required_elements: (i) $[\text{HAsc}] = \frac{(0.0550\text{ M})(V_{eq})}{0.0100\text{ L}}$ where $V_{eq}$ is read from the graph.
alternatives.correct_example:
(i) At equivalence, $n(\text{NaOH}) = n(\text{HAsc})$: $[\text{HAsc}] = \frac{(0.0550)(0.0080)}{0.0100} = 0.044\text{ M}$ 
(ii) p$K_a \approx 4.7$ (pH at half-equivalence point from graph) 
(iii) Henderson–Hasselbalch: $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log\frac{[\text{Asc}^-]}{[\text{HAsc}]}$; when pH = 4.7 = p$K_a$, $\log\frac{[\text{Asc}^-]}{[\text{HAsc}]} = 0$, so ratio = 1.

wrong_examples: Reading the equivalence point volume incorrectly from the graph.
Reporting the ratio as 0 instead of 1 when pH = pKa.
common_traps: Using pH at the equivalence point rather than the half-equivalence point to find pKa.

i. The rate law for the reaction is rate = k HAsc I 3 - . Explain how the data in the table
7 A7 A
HAsc
support the conclusion that the reaction is first order with respect to .
ii. Calculate the value of the rate constant, k, for the reaction. Include units with your

parts.scoring_notes: Part (c)(i): compare trials 1 and 3 (same [I₃⁻], [HAsc] doubles, rate doubles → first order in HAsc). Part (c)(ii): use any trial; k = rate/([HAsc][I₃⁻]); units = M⁻¹s⁻¹.

scoring_points.description: For a correct explanation of first-order dependence on HAsc using data: 1 point

required_elements: Comparing trials 1 and 3: [I₃⁻] is constant; [HAsc] doubles from 0.450 to 0.900 M; rate also doubles from 2.457×10⁻⁴ to 4.914×10⁻⁴ M/s.
A doubling of [HAsc] that doubles the rate is consistent with first-order dependence on HAsc.
alternatives.correct_example: Comparing trials 1 and 3: $[\text{I}_3^-]$ is held constant at 1.200 M while $[\text{HAsc}]$ doubles (0.450 → 0.900 M). The rate also doubles ($2.457 \times 10^{-4}$ → $4.914 \times 10^{-4}$ M/s). Since doubling the concentration of HAsc doubles the rate, the reaction is first order with respect to HAsc.

wrong_examples: Comparing trials 1 and 2 to argue for HAsc order — those trials vary I₃⁻, not HAsc.
common_traps: Using the wrong pair of trials; to isolate the HAsc order, must hold [I₃⁻] constant.

scoring_points.description: For the correct numerical value of k with correct units (M⁻¹s⁻¹): 1 point
wrong_examples: Reporting units as s⁻¹ (first-order units) for a second-order rate constant.
Dividing rate by only one concentration.
common_traps: For a second-order overall rate law (first order in each reactant), units of k are M⁻¹s⁻¹.

parts.prompt:
The triiodide ion, I 3 , is significantly more soluble in water than elemental iodine, $I_2$, is.
-
Identify an intermolecular force between I 3 and water that is not present between $I_2$ and
-
water, which could explain the difference in solubility. Lewis diagrams for $I_2$ and I 3 are
provided.
parts.scoring_notes: Accept 'ion–dipole' or 'ion-dipole interactions/attractions' only. Do not accept hydrogen bonding.

scoring_points.description: For identifying ion–dipole attractions as the intermolecular force unique to I₃⁻/water: 1 point

required_elements: Ion–dipole attractions exist between I₃⁻ (an ion) and the polar water molecules.
I₂ is a nonpolar molecule and can only interact with water via weak London dispersion forces, not ion–dipole.
alternatives.correct_example: Ion–dipole attractions are present between $\text{I}_3^-$ ions and the polar water molecules but are not present between nonpolar $\text{I}_2$ molecules and water. Ion–dipole forces are much stronger than the dispersion forces that $\text{I}_2$ can exert, explaining the greater solubility of $\text{I}_3^-$.

wrong_examples: Stating hydrogen bonding — neither I₃⁻ nor I₂ can form hydrogen bonds with water through the halogen.
Stating dipole–dipole — I₃⁻ is an ion, not a polar molecule.
common_traps: Confusing ion–dipole with dipole–dipole; the former requires at least one ion.
